retrieved_docs.title: Bortezomib -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Bortezomib -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Bortezomib is a proteasome inhibitor. The proteasomal system plays a vital role in cellular protein turnover, which is essential for the homeostasis of cells. Bortezomib reversibly binds to the chymotrypsin-like subunit of the 26S proteasome, resulting in its inhibition and preventing the degradation of various pro-apoptotic factors. [4] [5] The accumulation will eventually activate the programmed cell death via caspase-mediated pathways in the neoplastic cells that are usually dependent on the suppression of pro-apoptotic pathways for their proliferation and survival.

retrieved_docs.title: Bortezomib -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Summarize the mechanism of action of bortezomib. Identify the approved indications for bortezomib. Review the adverse events of bortezomib. Outline interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination and communication to advance bortezomib use and improve outcomes in cancer treatment. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Bortezomib -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: It is through this type of interprofessional effort between physicians, nurses, and pharmacists that bortezomib therapy can achieve optimal therapeutic outcomes for patients while minimizing the potential for adverse events. [Level 5]

retrieved_docs.title: Bortezomib -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: The drug is not recommended for use in women who are pregnant or breastfeeding. There is a definite risk of harm to the fetus if used during pregnancy. If the female is pregnant and has a life-threatening situation, the use of bortezomib requires a thorough discussion with the patient, oncologists, and members of the ethics committee.

retrieved_docs.title: Bortezomib -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Bortezomib is a drug for the treatment of multiple myeloma. Because of its narrow therapeutic index, the drug is used only by a few healthcare professionals. Ordering should be under the direction of an oncology specialist who is well-versed in the use of the drug.  The drug is administered parenterally and requires close monitoring by the nursing staff. Besides checking the CBC, the liver function involves assessment. Before starting the drug, a baseline chest X-ray is recommended.

retrieved_docs.title: Bortezomib -- Administration
retrieved_docs.content: For mantle cell lymphoma, bortezomib is dosed at 1.3 mg/m^2 x 1 IV on days 1, 4, 8, and 11 of a 21-day treatment cycle, with a minimum of 72 hours between doses. Avoid injecting at the same site within a single cycle. The drug is metabolized by the CYP3A4 and CYP2C19 enzymes in the liver. The metabolites are inactive and are excreted by the kidney.

retrieved_docs.title: Bortezomib -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Bortezomib is FDA-approved for use in the initial treatment of multiple myeloma in combination with cyclophosphamide and dexamethasone. It is also FDA-approved for use in the treatment of multiple myeloma in individuals who previously responded to bortezomib and relapsed at least six months after the completion of the prior treatment. Also, it is FDA-approved for use in the treatment of mantle cell lymphoma in individuals who have received at least one previous first-line treatment. This activity will review the mechanism of action, adverse event profile, toxicity, dosing, pharmacodynamics, and monitoring pertinent for members of the interprofessional team for the treatment of patients with multiple myeloma.

retrieved_docs.title: Bortezomib -- Toxicity
retrieved_docs.content: Bortezomib should be withheld at the onset of grade 3 non-hematological or grade 4 hematological toxicity until the toxicity resolves. Treatment is then treatment restarted at a lower adjusted dose. There is no antidote available to reverse the toxic effects of the drug.

retrieved_docs.title: Bortezomib -- Monitoring -- Special Patient Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Elderly: While data are lacking, one should take great precautions when administering bortezomib to seniors because of the risk of toxicity. However, the effect of the drug is the same as in younger patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Bortezomib -- Administration
retrieved_docs.content: Bortezomib is available in intravenous (IV) and subcutaneous (SQ) forms. There is no difference in the response rates between the available forms; however, the SQ form correlates with a decreased occurrence of grade 3 or higher adverse effects. IV form administration is as a rapid push (3 to 5 seconds). The dosing regimens below will reference IV administration.

retrieved_docs.title: Bortezomib -- Administration
retrieved_docs.content: For previously untreated multiple myeloma, bortezomib dosing is 1.3 mg/m^2 X 1 IV on days 1, 4, 8, 11, 22, 25, 29, and 32 of a 42-day cycle for the first 4 cycles; doses must be at least 72 hours apart. Subsequent dosing is 1.3 mg/m^2 X 1 IV on days 1, 8, 22, and 29 of a 42-day cycle. Bortezomib is given as part of a multi-drug regimen.

retrieved_docs.title: Bortezomib -- Administration
retrieved_docs.content: For previously relapsing multiple myeloma, bortezomib dosing is 1.3 mg/m^2 X 1 IV on days 1, 4, 8, and 11 of a 21-day cycle. If the patient has previously received bortezomib therapy, give up to 8 cycles, then starting with cycle 9, dosing can be 1.3 mg/m^2 X 1 IV on days 1, 8, 15, and 22 of a 35-day cycle; doses must be at least 72 hours apart. In this regimen, bortezomib can be given with dexamethasone or as monotherapy. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Bortezomib -- Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: Frequently check CBC with differential and platelets during the therapy as it causes myelosuppression and pancytopenia. Cell counts reach a nadir by day 11, and recovery occurs by day 21. Check liver function tests in patients with existing hepatic impairment as it can lead to hepatic failure or abscess formation. Avoid using the drug if liver enzymes increase to 2 to 3 times the upper limit of normal. Evaluate for signs/symptoms of peripheral neuropathy. Frequently assess the volume status and check orthostatic blood pressure during the treatment. Order a baseline chest x-ray and then periodic pulmonary function testing with new or worsening pulmonary symptoms. [13] [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Bortezomib -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Bortezomib is FDA-approved for use in the initial treatment of multiple myeloma in combination with cyclophosphamide and dexamethasone. It is also FDA-approved for use in the treatment of multiple myeloma in individuals who previously responded to bortezomib and relapsed at least six months after the completion of the prior treatment. Also, it is FDA-approved for use in the treatment of mantle cell lymphoma in individuals who have received at least one prior first-line treatment. [1] [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1981
retrieved_docs.content: Inhibitory proteas refers to a class of proteasome‑inhibiting agents. Ixazomib is a specific drug that belongs to this inhibitory proteas class, acting as a reversible proteasome inhibitor used in the treatment of multiple myeloma. Remikiren, although primarily recognized as a renin inhibitor, is also categorized within the inhibitory proteas group because it exhibits proteasome‑inhibitory activity, placing it alongside ixazomib as another member of this therapeutic class. This relationship highlights that both ixazomib and remikiren share a common mechanism of proteasome inhibition, despite their differing primary indications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 679
retrieved_docs.content: Protease inhibitors, direct acting antivirals constitute a class of antiviral agents that target viral proteases. Key members of this class include the protease inhibitors ritonavir, fosamprenavir, amprenavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir. Amprenavir is linked to a downstream entity termed inhibitory proteas, reflecting a broader relationship between antiviral protease inhibition and proteasome inhibition. Inhibitory proteas is classified as ixazomib, a proteasome inhibitor used in the treatment of multiple myeloma. This connection highlights the pharmacologic overlap where agents originally developed as viral protease inhibitors share mechanistic features with proteasome‑targeting drugs, informing both antiviral therapy and oncology drug development.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1817
retrieved_docs.content: Hirudiny is identified as an inhibitory proteas, and inhibitory proteas are classified as a type of ixazomib. Consequently, Hirudiny can be considered a member of the ixazomib class of proteasome‑inhibiting agents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2594
retrieved_docs.content: Amprenavir is linked to the concept of inhibitory proteas as a broader category that includes more specific proteasome‑inhibiting agents. The entity inhibitory proteas is identified as ixazomib, indicating that ixazomib is a particular proteasome inhibitor falling under this category. Additionally, remikiren is classified as a type of inhibitory proteas, meaning that remikiren also belongs to the same class of proteasome‑inhibiting compounds associated with ixazomib. Together, amprenavir, inhibitory proteas, ixazomib, and remikiren illustrate a hierarchical relationship among proteasome inhibitors, with amprenavir encompassing the broader class, inhibitory proteas representing the specific class, and ixazomib and remikiren being individual agents within that class.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 1936
retrieved_docs.content: Protease inhibitors that function as direct‑acting antivirals comprise a pharmacologic group that includes ritonavir, tipranavir, amprenavir and atazanavir. These agents are linked not only by their ability to inhibit viral proteases but also by a broader association with proteasome inhibition pathways. Amprenavir, in particular, is connected to a downstream entity described as “inhibitory proteas,” indicating that it may give rise to or be related to proteasome‑inhibiting activity. The “inhibitory proteas” concept itself is classified under the proteasome inhibitor ixazomib, and the peptide‑derived compound hirudiny is identified as an instance of this inhibitory proteas class. Together, these relationships highlight a network in which several antiviral protease inhibitors intersect with proteasome‑targeting mechanisms, suggesting potential overlapping therapeutic effects or shared molecular targets relevant to antiviral and anticancer drug development.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 1174
retrieved_docs.content: Protease inhibitors, direct acting antivirals constitute a pharmacologic class that includes the antiviral agents ritonavir, fosamprenavir, amprenavir, atazanavir and tipranavir. Within this class, amprenavir is linked to a subcategory termed inhibitory proteas, and the broader protease‑inhibitor class is also associated with the inhibitory proteas concept. Inhibitory proteas is identified as the proteasome‑inhibiting drug ixazomib, and it encompasses the specific agent remikiren. These hierarchical and functional connections illustrate how antiviral protease inhibitors relate to proteasome‑targeting compounds, underscoring shared mechanistic themes that are important for drug classification, therapeutic decision‑making and the development of treatment strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2174
retrieved_docs.content: Hirudiny is classified as an inhibitory proteas, indicating that it functions as a proteasome‑inhibiting agent. The class of inhibitory proteas is linked to ixazomib, a well‑known proteasome inhibitor drug, and is also related to remikiren, which is identified as a member of this inhibitory proteas category. Together, hirudiny, ixazomib, and remikiren share the common mechanism of proteasome inhibition, a therapeutic strategy employed in conditions such as certain cancers where disruption of protein degradation pathways can be beneficial.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1825
retrieved_docs.content: Amprenavir is connected to an entity called inhibitory proteas, which is classified as ixazomib; thus, the inhibitory proteas component associated with amprenavir is identified as the proteasome‑inhibiting drug ixazomib.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 477
retrieved_docs.content: Protease inhibitors that are classified as direct‑acting antivirals form a therapeutic group that includes fosamprenavir, amprenavir, indinavir and tipranavir. Within this group, amprenavir is linked to the concept of inhibitory proteas, indicating that it possesses or is associated with proteasome‑inhibitory activity. More generally, protease inhibitors as a class have a broader relationship with inhibitory proteas, suggesting overlapping mechanisms between antiviral protease inhibition and proteasome inhibition. The inhibitory proteas category itself comprises agents such as ixazomib and the compound hirudiny, both of which act as proteasome inhibitors. Recognizing these interconnections helps clinicians understand how antiviral protease inhibitors may intersect with proteasome‑targeting drugs, informing decisions about combination therapy, potential cross‑reactivity, and management of adverse effects.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 402
retrieved_docs.content: Protease inhibitors, direct acting antivirals constitute a therapeutic class that includes the specific agents fosamprenavir, atazanavir and tipranavir, each of which is a member of this antiviral protease‑inhibiting group. Beyond their antiviral activity, protease inhibitors share a broader pharmacologic relationship with compounds that act as inhibitory proteas, linking viral protease inhibition to proteasome inhibition pathways. Within the inhibitory proteas category, both hirudiny and ixazomib function as proteasome‑inhibiting agents, illustrating that they are types of inhibitory proteas. Remikiren is associated with the inhibitory proteas class through an inverse relationship, suggesting that it is not a direct member but rather a molecule whose activity is counter‑regulated or targeted by proteasome inhibition. This network of relationships underscores the overlap between antiviral protease inhibition and proteasome inhibition, providing insight into shared mechanisms that may influence drug selection, combination therapy, and potential repurposing strategies in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 619
retrieved_docs.content: Protease inhibitors that are classified as direct‑acting antivirals comprise several specific agents, including fosamprenavir, amprenavir and tipranavir. These antiviral protease inhibitors are closely linked to the broader category of proteasome‑inhibiting compounds (referred to as “inhibitory proteas”), indicating that their mechanism of action involves inhibition of proteasomal activity. Within this inhibitory proteas group, amprenavir is described as having a child relationship to the inhibitory proteas class, reinforcing its role as a proteasome‑targeting drug, and the protease‑inhibitor class as a whole is said to have a broader relationship with inhibitory proteas. Additional members of the inhibitory proteas family include the natural peptide hirudiny and the synthetic agent ixazomib, both of which are recognized as proteasome inhibitors. In terms of drug safety, amprenavir is contraindicated for use with cisapride monohydrate, meaning that co‑administration of these two agents should be avoided. Cisapride monohydrate itself is also listed as a contraindicated class for furosemide (furosemid), indicating that these medications should not be combined. Recognizing these contraindications is essential for preventing adverse drug interactions and ensuring safe patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1982
retrieved_docs.content: Amprenavir, hirudiny, ixazomib, and remikiren are all connected through the concept of inhibitory proteas (proteasome inhibition). Amprenavir has a child entity identified as inhibitory proteas, indicating that a proteasome‑inhibiting component is derived from or associated with amprenavir. Hirudiny is classified as an inhibitory proteas, meaning it functions as a proteasome inhibitor. The inhibitory proteas class is linked to ixazomib, a clinically used proteasome inhibitor, suggesting that inhibitory proteas share mechanistic or structural characteristics with ixazomib. Additionally, remikiren is positioned as a subordinate entity within the inhibitory proteas hierarchy, indicating that remikiren is a specific proteasome‑inhibiting agent related to the broader inhibitory proteas category. Together, these relationships illustrate how various drugs and compounds are organized around the central pharmacologic theme of proteasome inhibition, which is relevant for therapeutic strategies targeting protein degradation pathways.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 893
retrieved_docs.content: Protease inhibitors that are direct‑acting antivirals constitute a therapeutic class that includes fosamprenavir, amprenavir, indinavir, atazanavir and tipranavir. This class is broadly linked to the concept of “inhibitory proteas,” indicating a relationship with proteasome inhibition. Amprenavir, in particular, is described as having a child entity “inhibitory proteas,” reinforcing its connection to proteasome‑targeting activity. The “inhibitory proteas” category itself comprises agents such as ixazomib and the peptide hirudiny, both of which are identified as proteasome inhibitors. In the oncology setting, the FOLFIRI antineoplastic chemotherapy regimen is directly associated with the chemotherapeutic agent irinotecan (spelled “irinotekan” in the source data). Atazanavir, one of the protease inhibitors, carries a contraindication for use with irinotecan, highlighting a potential drug‑interaction risk when these agents are co‑administered. Together, these relationships underscore the interconnected nature of antiviral protease inhibition, proteasome inhibition, and chemotherapy agents, and they emphasize the need for careful medication reconciliation to avoid adverse interactions in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 2 -- Community 1221
retrieved_docs.content: Protease inhibitors, direct acting antivirals constitute a class of antiviral agents that target viral proteases. Members of this class include ritonavir, fosamprenavir, amprenavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir. Amprenavir is specifically linked to the concept of inhibitory proteas, indicating that it may possess proteasome‑inhibiting activity or be a precursor to such agents. The protease‑inhibitor class as a whole is also described as having a broader relationship with inhibitory proteas, suggesting overlap between viral protease inhibition and proteasome inhibition mechanisms. Compounds that are classified as inhibitory proteas include hirudiny and the approved proteasome inhibitor ixazomib. Remikiren is related to inhibitory proteas in an inverse hierarchical manner, implying that it represents a broader or distinct category that encompasses proteasome‑inhibiting agents. Together, these relationships highlight the interconnected pharmacologic landscape of viral protease inhibitors and proteasome inhibitors, which can be relevant for designing combination therapies and understanding cross‑reactivity in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2309
retrieved_docs.content: Amprenavir, a medication primarily known as an HIV protease inhibitor, is linked to a derivative or “child” entity described as an inhibitory proteasome agent, indicating that a form of amprenavir may possess proteasome‑inhibiting activity. Hirudiny is also identified as belonging to the class of inhibitory proteasome agents, suggesting that it functions as a proteasome inhibitor. The category of inhibitory proteasome agents encompasses ixazomib, a specific oral proteasome inhibitor used in the treatment of multiple myeloma. Together, these relationships illustrate that amprenavir, hirudiny, and ixazomib are interconnected through their association with proteasome inhibition, a therapeutic mechanism that interferes with protein degradation pathways and has relevance in both antiviral and anticancer strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 379
retrieved_docs.content: Cisapride and its monohydrate formulation are identified as contraindicated drug classes for both furosemide and fluconazole, indicating that co‑administration of these agents should be avoided due to potential adverse interactions. Amprenavir similarly exhibits contraindicated class relationships with cisapride and cisapride monohydrate, reinforcing the need for careful medication reconciliation when these antivirals are prescribed. In addition, amprenavir is linked to an “inhibitory proteas” child entity, suggesting a pharmacologic or metabolic connection to proteasome‑inhibiting mechanisms. The inhibitory proteas class itself is positioned hierarchically as a subclass of the proteasome inhibitor ixazomib, while remikiren is a subclass of inhibitory proteas, establishing a lineage of related proteasome‑targeting agents. Hirudiny is also classified within the inhibitory proteas family, further expanding the network of compounds that share this mechanism of action. Recognizing these contraindications and hierarchical relationships is essential for optimizing drug selection, preventing harmful drug‑drug interactions, and guiding therapeutic strategies that involve proteasome inhibition.
